name: Prophet Muhammad
shortDescription: Prophet Muhammad is considered the final prophet in Islam, believed to have received divine revelations from Allah through the angel Jibreel (Gabriel). He is revered as the founder of Islam and the restorer of the original monotheistic faith of Adam, Abraham, Moses, and Jesus.
description: Prophet Muhammad, born in Mecca around 570 CE, is the central figure of Islam and widely regarded as the last prophet sent by God (Allah). According to Islamic belief, he received revelations from Allah through the angel Jibreel (Gabriel) starting around the age of 40. These revelations, recorded in the Quran, form the basis of Islamic scripture. Muhammad's life and teachings, as documented in the Hadith (sayings and actions), provide a model for Muslims to follow. His journey, known as the Hijra, from Mecca to Medina in 622 CE marks the beginning of the Islamic calendar and a turning point in the establishment of the Muslim community. He unified most of Arabia under a single religious and political system by the time of his death in 632 CE. Muslims believe that Muhammad restored the original, uncorrupted monotheistic faith of earlier prophets like Abraham, Moses, and Jesus. While not considered divine himself, he is revered as the perfect example of human conduct and submission to God's will. The Shahada, the Islamic declaration of faith, affirms that "There is no god but Allah, and Muhammad is his messenger."
symbols: Sword (Zulfiqar, though more associated with Ali)

sources.title: Muhammad: A Prophet for Our Time
sources.author: Karen Armstrong

worshipPractices.name: Salat (Prayer)
worshipPractices.description: Muslims perform five daily prayers facing the Kaaba in Mecca, following specific rituals and reciting verses from the Quran. These prayers are a direct connection with Allah and a fundamental pillar of Islam. They are seen as a way to cleanse the heart and seek guidance.

worshipPractices.name: Following the Sunnah
worshipPractices.description: Muslims strive to emulate the Prophet Muhammad's actions, sayings, and character, known as the Sunnah. This includes following his example in matters of personal hygiene, family life, social interactions, and ethical conduct. The Sunnah provides a practical guide to living a righteous life according to Islamic principles.

worshipPractices.name: Sending Blessings Upon Him
worshipPractices.description: Muslims are encouraged to send blessings and peace upon the Prophet Muhammad, often by reciting "Allahumma salli ala Muhammad" (O Allah, send blessings upon Muhammad). This act expresses love, respect, and gratitude for his role as a messenger of God. It is believed to bring blessings to the one who recites it.

festivals.name: Eid al-Milad (The Prophet's Birthday)
festivals.timing: 12th day of Rabi' al-Awwal (third month in the Islamic calendar)
festivals.description: Eid al-Milad is a celebration of the Prophet Muhammad's birth. Celebrations vary among different Muslim communities, but often include religious lectures, singing devotional songs (nasheeds), and distributing food to the poor. Some communities also decorate homes and mosques with lights.

cultural.modernLegacy: Prophet Muhammad's teachings and example continue to inspire Muslims worldwide in various aspects of life, from personal conduct to social justice initiatives. His life story is frequently depicted in literature, films, and art, serving as a source of moral guidance and cultural identity for millions. Islamic art and architecture often feature calligraphy of his name or sayings, reflecting the deep reverence he holds in Islamic culture.

cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: Both Moses and Muhammad are considered prophets who received divine revelations and laws that formed the basis of their respective religions; Moses received the Torah on Mount Sinai, while Muhammad received the Quran through the angel Gabriel.

Both Zarathushtra and Muhammad are seen as founders of monotheistic religions who received divine messages and reformed existing religious practices; both emphasized ethical monotheism and a final judgment.

While not directly analogous in terms of divine revelation, both Muhammad and Heracles are figures who overcame significant challenges and opposition to establish a new order or way of life; Heracles through his labors and Muhammad through his religious and political leadership.

associations.name: Moon
associations.significance: The moon symbolizes guidance and enlightenment, reflecting the divine light and illuminating the path for believers, mirroring Muhammad's role as a guide.

associations.name: Justice
associations.significance: Muhammad is strongly associated with establishing justice and fairness, both in his personal dealings and as the leader of the early Muslim community.

associations.name: One
associations.significance: Represents the concept of Tawhid (Oneness of God), which is the central message of Islam that Muhammad preached.

associations.name: Green
associations.significance: Green is traditionally associated with paradise and represents growth, life, and the flourishing of Islam under Muhammad's guidance.

associations.name: Camel
associations.significance: The camel represents resilience, endurance, and the journey across the desert, mirroring Muhammad's arduous journey and perseverance in spreading his message.

associations.name: Mercy
associations.significance: Muhammad is often referred to as a mercy to mankind, embodying compassion and forgiveness in his teachings and actions.

associations.name: Date Palm
associations.significance: The date palm is a symbol of sustenance, blessing, and the fertile lands of Arabia, representing the blessings brought forth by Muhammad's message.

companions.name: Jibreel (Gabriel)
companions.description: The archangel who delivered divine revelations from Allah to Prophet Muhammad, serving as a crucial intermediary.

companions.name: Abu Bakr
companions.description: A close companion and the first caliph of Islam after Muhammad's death. He was known for his unwavering loyalty and support.

companions.name: Ali ibn Abi Talib
companions.description: Muhammad's cousin and son-in-law, considered a key figure in Shia Islam and the fourth caliph in Sunni Islam. He was known for his wisdom and bravery.

companions.name: Buraq
companions.description: A mythical steed, described as a winged creature, that transported Prophet Muhammad during the Isra and Mi'raj (Night Journey). It symbolizes the miraculous journey to Jerusalem and ascent to heaven.

companions.name: Khadija bint Khuwaylid
companions.description: Muhammad's first wife and a strong supporter of his prophethood. She was a successful businesswoman who provided him with emotional and financial stability.

extendedContent.title: Origins & Birth: Miraculous Signs and Divine Protection
extendedContent.content:
The birth of Prophet Muhammad is surrounded by miraculous events and divine portents, signifying his special status. Islamic tradition recounts that his mother, Aminah, experienced a pregnancy without pain and saw a light emanating from her that illuminated the palaces of Syria. Pre-Islamic soothsayers and Jewish scholars in Medina reportedly predicted the arrival of a prophet from Mecca, further emphasizing the anticipation surrounding his birth. Some accounts mention the extinguishing of the Persian fire, a sacred flame revered by Zoroastrians, as a sign of the impending triumph of Islam. These narratives, while not explicitly mentioned in the Quran, are prevalent in hadith literature and sira (biographies of Muhammad), highlighting the belief that his arrival was divinely orchestrated.

Furthermore, legends emphasize divine protection during his childhood. Stories recount how the angel Gabriel cleansed Muhammad's heart, removing a black clot representing worldly desires, and filling it with faith and wisdom. This episode, known as the 'شق الصدر' (splitting of the breast), is interpreted as a symbolic purification, preparing him for his prophetic mission. The death of his parents early in his life is also seen as part of a divine plan, placing him under the care of his grandfather and uncle, who belonged to the respected tribe of Quraysh, thus safeguarding him from potential harm and nurturing him within a noble lineage.

These narratives surrounding his birth and childhood contribute to the understanding of Muhammad as a divinely chosen individual, set apart from ordinary humans. They reinforce the belief that his life was guided and protected by Allah, paving the way for his pivotal role in the revelation of the Quran and the establishment of Islam. The emphasis on miraculous signs and divine intervention serves to validate his prophethood and inspire faith among believers.

extendedContent.title: The Night Journey (Isra and Mi'raj): Ascension to Divine Presence
extendedContent.content:
The Isra and Mi'raj, the Prophet Muhammad's miraculous night journey and ascension to heaven, is a pivotal event in Islamic mythology and theology. According to tradition, around 621 CE, Muhammad was transported from Mecca to Jerusalem (Isra) on a winged steed called Buraq. In Jerusalem, he led all the previous prophets in prayer, symbolically affirming his status as the final prophet in the Abrahamic lineage. This event is briefly mentioned in Surah Al-Isra (17:1) of the Quran: 'Exalted is He who took His Servant by night from al-Masjid al-Haram to al-Masjid al-Aqsa, whose surroundings We have blessed, to show him of Our signs. Indeed, He is the Hearing, the Seeing.'

From Jerusalem, Muhammad ascended through the seven heavens (Mi'raj), encountering prophets such as Adam, Moses, and Jesus at different levels. He ultimately reached the Divine Presence, a realm beyond human comprehension, where he received instructions regarding the daily prayers (Salat) for Muslims. The specifics of this encounter are largely detailed in hadith literature and mystical interpretations. Some accounts describe Muhammad's conversation with Allah, while others emphasize the indescribable nature of the experience, highlighting the limits of human language to capture divine reality.

The Isra and Mi'raj holds profound theological significance. It demonstrates Muhammad's unique spiritual status and his direct connection to Allah. The establishment of the five daily prayers during this journey underscores their importance as a fundamental pillar of Islam. The event also serves as a testament to the power and majesty of Allah, who transcends the limitations of the physical world. The night journey is commemorated annually by Muslims and serves as a reminder of the spiritual potential within each individual to strive for closeness to God.

extendedContent.title: Muhammad as the Seal of the Prophets (Khatam an-Nabiyyin)
extendedContent.content:
A central tenet in Islamic belief is that Prophet Muhammad is the 'Seal of the Prophets' (Khatam an-Nabiyyin), meaning he is the last prophet sent by God. This concept is explicitly stated in the Quran (Surah Al-Ahzab 33:40): 'Muhammad is not the father of [any] of your men, but [he is] the Messenger of Allah and last of the prophets. And ever is Allah, of all things, Knowing.' This verse is interpreted as signifying the completion and perfection of divine revelation through Muhammad. It implies that no prophet will come after him to bring a new religion or supersede the teachings of Islam.

The concept of 'Khatam an-Nabiyyin' doesn't necessarily negate the possibility of 'awliya' (saints or friends of God) or righteous individuals who receive divine inspiration. However, it clearly distinguishes between prophethood, which involves receiving direct revelation from Allah and establishing a new religious law, and other forms of spiritual guidance or inspiration. Islamic scholars interpret the 'seal' metaphor to mean that Muhammad's message confirms and completes the messages of previous prophets, such as Abraham, Moses, and Jesus.

The belief in Muhammad as the last prophet has significant implications for Islamic theology and practice. It reinforces the finality and completeness of the Quran as the ultimate guide for humanity. It also emphasizes the importance of adhering to the Sunnah (the practices and teachings of Muhammad) as a means of understanding and implementing the Quranic principles. The 'Khatam an-Nabiyyin' doctrine safeguards against the emergence of new religions or prophets claiming to supersede Islam, preserving the integrity and unity of the Muslim community.

extendedContent.title: Iconography & Representation: Aniconism and Symbolic Presence
extendedContent.content:
Islam generally prohibits the depiction of God (Allah) and Prophet Muhammad in visual form. This aniconism, derived from interpretations of the Quran and hadith, aims to prevent idolatry and maintain the unique and transcendent nature of the divine. While there are historical and regional variations in the observance of this prohibition, the dominant view within Sunni Islam discourages realistic portrayals of Muhammad. This is not to say there is a complete absence of imagery. In some Shia communities, particularly in Iran, depictions of Muhammad exist, though they are often stylized and respectful, avoiding any suggestion of deification.

Instead of visual representations, Islamic art and culture emphasize calligraphy as a means of expressing reverence for Muhammad. The writing of his name in beautiful scripts, such as Thuluth or Kufic, is a common motif in mosques, homes, and other sacred spaces. These calligraphic representations serve as a symbolic presence, reminding believers of Muhammad's teachings and inspiring devotion. The recitation of salawat (blessings upon Muhammad) is another important practice, verbalizing love and respect for the prophet.

The absence of realistic depictions of Muhammad has shaped Islamic artistic expression, fostering a focus on abstract patterns, geometric designs, and the beauty of the Arabic language. It also encourages a deeper engagement with the Quran and hadith, as Muslims are encouraged to learn about Muhammad's life and teachings through these primary sources rather than relying on potentially misleading or incomplete visual representations. This focus on textual and oral traditions reinforces the importance of knowledge and understanding in Islamic spirituality.
